matched_title: Olympics news: Imane Khelif row takes fresh twist as Team GB win three gold medals | Other | Sport | Express.co.uk
full_text:
Team GBwill be desperate to add to their growing tally of gold medals as theOlympicsenters its seventh day. There were no British golds on Thursday but the women's four secured silver and there were bronze medals in both the men's four and the women's double sculls.

Another action-packed schedule on Friday will present gold medal opportunities in swimming, rowing and diving. Emily Craig and Imogen Grant are the big favourites in the women’s lightweight double sculls after breezing through their heats, with the final set to take place before midday.

The athletics events will also get underway with Keely Hodgkinson among the main contenders in the women's 800 metres. Elsewhere at the Games, swimming sensation Leon Marchand is looking to complete a perfect home Olympics as he targets a fourth gold in the pool.

Follow Express Sport's live updates below...

Discovery+is the new home of the Olympics. The streamer is set to broadcast 3,800 hours of sport live from Paris.

Sports fans can getDiscovery+ for £3.99a month until the end of 2024 via Amazon Prime's video channels. This also comes with anAmazon Prime 30-day free trial.

That's another dramatic day at the Olympics for Team GB, and one that brought three more gold medals.

We'll be back from early tomorrow with all the latest news and results.

France set up an Olympic men's football semi-final with Egypt by beating Argentina 1-0 ibut the game was marred by ugly scenes at the final whistle.

Players and coaches from both sides clashed as animosity spilled over. Crystal Palace striker Jean-Philippe Mateta got France's winner when he headed home Michael Olise's fifth-minute corner.



The Czech Republic have become the first nation to secure a gold medal at the tennis in Paris.

Boyfriend and girlfriend Tomas Machac and Katerina Siniakova beat Xinyu Wang and Zhizhen Zhang of China in a deciding tiebreak, 6-2 5-7 [10-8].

There was speculation that the Czech stars broke up ahead of the tournament, but they shared a kiss after winning gold.

What a night so far for the hosts! Swimming, BMX, and maybe now football.

In the quarter-final, they lead Argentina 1-0 with just 15 minutes left.

Crystal Palace striker Jean-Philippe Mateta has the only goal sofar, heading in after only five minutes.

Beth Shriever just had a terrible start in the BMX racing final, and finished last.

Gold goes to Australia's Saya Sakakibara, with Manon Veenstra of the Netherlands taking silver. Switzerland's Zoe Claessens won bronze.

The champion from Tokyo got caught up in early traffic and couldn't recover.

It followed Kye White suffering a horror crash to end his hopes in the men's final.

READ MORE HERE.

Novak Djokovic is now just one game away from a first ever Olympic gold medal.

The world No.2 beat Lorenzo Musetti 6-4 6-2 and will faceCarlos Alcarazon Sunday’s - a rematch of theWimbledonfinal.

READ MORE HERE.

Two silvers in a matter of minutes for Team GB!

Duncan Scott comes second in men's 200m individual medley. Home hero Leon Marchand wins it in a raucous atmosphere, a fourth gold of the Games.

That's EIGHT Olympic medals for Scott. A Team GB legend.

Ben Proud takes the silver medal in the men's 50m freestyle.

Australia's Cam McEvoy took gold and Florent Manaudou of France claimed bronze. A thrilling race.



Keely Hodgkinson wins her 800m heat with room to spare.

She is now safely through to the semi-finals, which take place on Sunday. Another Team GB member, 17-year-old Phoebe Gill, will join her.

Team GB are safely into the next round of the Mixed 4x400m relay with Sam Reardon, Laviai Nielsen, Alex Haydock-Wilson and Nicole Yeargin setting a new British record.

In the previous heat however, the Amerixan quartet set a new world record. Expect another one tomorrow too.

Here comes Novak Djokovic to take on Italy's Lorenzo Musetti.

The winner of course, faces Carlos Alcaraz in the final. A remonder that Novak Djokovic has never won Olympic gold....

So sit tight for the evening folks, here are the main gold medal hopes for Team GB.....

7.30pm:Men's 50m Freestyle Final (Ben Proud);7.36pm:Women's 200m Backstroke Final (Katie Shanahan, Honey Osrin);7.43pm:Men's 200m Individual Medley Final (Duncan Scott),8.35pm:BMX Racing Men's Final (Kye Whyte);8.50pm:BMX Racing Women's Final (Beth Shriver).

Relaxed? No, us neither.

It's gold for Teddy Riner in the men's 100kg category!

The Frenchman sinks to his knees after defeating Kim Min-jong of South Korea and his know the most successful judoka in Olympic history.

He has four gold medals and two bronzes, and the 35-year-old could add another tomorrow in the mixed team event.

There was drama in the women's 100 metres heats after South Sudan athlete Lucia Moris fell before screaming in pain.

But she was attended to by a rival before being stretchered away.

READ MORE HERE.



There's the men's kayak cross time trials done and dusted.

Joe Clarke, who agonisingly missed out on the medals in the kayak singles yesterday, has qualified for the first round of the kayak cross in the quickest time.

Clarke notched 66.08 seconds. Team GB's Adam Burgess goes through in 26th place.



World Sailing have confirmed today's windsurfing men's and women's finals have been postponed until tomorrow.

Britain's Emma Wilson is in gold medal position in the women's table, but her date with destiny has been put on hold.

Team GB have won their third gold medal of the day with a brilliant triumph in the team showjumping event.

Ben Maher, Harry Charles and Scott Brash have emerged victorious in dramatic fashion to edge out the United States at the top of the standings.

It sees Team GB climb to third place in the overall medal table, putting them behind only the United States and leaders China.



Wimbledon champion Carlos Alcaraz is into the Olympic men's singles finalafter thrashing Felix Auger-Aliassime in straight sets.

He needed just 75 minutes to win their semi-final 6-1 6-1 to set up a potential showdown with Novak Djokovic for the gold medal.

If the Spaniard wins the top prize, he will become the youngest men's singles champion in history at the age of just 21.

Team GB gymnast Bryony Page has taken the gold medal with a stunning display in the women's trampoline final.

She went into her routine knowing she had to top Viyaleta Bardzilouskaya's score of 56.060 to take the lead and she did just that.

Her winning score of 56.48 sees Team GB claim their eighth gold medal of the Olympics and their second of the day.

Andy Murray was given a hero's welcome by his Team GB colleagues after playing his final ever match last night.

The two-time Wimbledon champion featured alongside Dan Evans in the men's doubles as they were knocked out by American duo Tommy Paul and Taylor Fritz.

A very warm welcome for a very special person. \u2764\ufe0fAndy Murray received a guard of honour from Team GB as he arrived back. \ud83e\udd1d#Paris2024#BBCOlympicspic.twitter.com/iBUA5PDRH0

Slovakian swimmer Tamara Potockawas carried away on a stretcher and given oxygenafter taking part in the women's 200-metre individual medley.

Her legs buckled as she climbed out of the pool before tumbling to the ground and being immediately swarmed by medics.

The 21-year-old was later taken away on a stretcher and was spotted with an oxygen mask as she left the arena.

The venue's media manager confirmed that Potocka was conscious and under medical assessment, according to Reuters.

It has since been reported byThe Athleticthat she suffered an asthma attack.

Emily Craig and Imogen Grant have taken gold for Team GB with a dominant display in the women's lightweight double sculls final.

They were somewhat slow to start but took the lead at the 400 metre mark and never looked back as they cruised to victory.

Romania have taken the silver medal with Greece finishing in third place.



Anthony Harding and Jack Laugher have won bronze for Team GB in the men's three-metre synchronised platform diving final.

Daoyi Long and Zongyuan Wang take gold for China with Mexican duo Juan Celaya and Osmar Olvera claiming the silver medal.

It was a very strong display from Harding and Laugher but nobody was going to catch China at the top.

Mexico also did brilliantly well to clinch silver with a series of outstanding dives.

Ollie Wynne-Griffith and Tom George have been forced to settle for silver in the men's pair final after being pipped to Olympic gold at the last second.

They looked set to secure Team GB's seventh gold medal of the Games until a late surge from Croatian brothers Valent and Martin Sinkovic saw them slip to second place.

Moe Sbihi, a former Olympic champion rower who was on commentary duties forBBCSport, said: "They put themselves in that hurt locker for so long in that race.

"They did 99 per cent of that right. It was just heartbreak for them. It's a great advert, again, for the last stroke that always counts."

Adam Peaty will not take part in the mixed 4x100m medley relay heats for Team GB this morning.

The three-time Olympic gold medallist tested positive for Covid-19 a day after winning silver in the 100m breaststroke.

He could still represent Team GB in the finals if they manage to qualify despite missing the heats.

Peaty could also compete in the men’s 4x100m medley relay on Saturday if he is deemed fit enough to take to the pool.



The only transgender boxer at the Olympics has called for fighters who fail gender tests to be banned.

It comes after Imane Khelif, who has XY chromosomes, sparked controversy in her 46-second win over Italian opponent Angela Carini.

Hergie Bacyadan, who identifies as a transgender man,said: "In sparring it's OK but if they have XY chromosomes in competition they should abide by the rules.

"I am trans at heart, but I did not take any hormones or steroids to look like a man. If I compete in the women's division, I don't think there's an issue."

American rapper Flavor Flav has paid Veronica Fraley's rent after the discus thrower revealed she was struggling to make ends meet despite being an Olympic athlete.

She wrote on social media: "I compete in the Olympic Games TOMORROW and can’t even pay my rent.

"My school only sent about 75 per cent of my rent while they pay football players (who haven’t won anything) enough to buy new cars and houses."

Flav, a founding member of Public Enemy, replied: ""I gotchu. DM me and I’ll send payment TODAY so you don’t have to worry about it TOMORROW and imma be rooting for ya tomorrow."

It was later reported byNBCthat Flav had paid Fraley's rent in full.

Here's a look at some of the biggest events taking place on day seven of the Games...

9.42am: Rowing - men's pair finals

9.54am: Rowing - women's pair finals

10.00am: Diving - men's 3km synchronised springboard final

10.06am: Rowing - men's lightweight double sculls finals

10.18am: Rowing - women's lightweight double sculls finals

12.50pm: Trampoline gymnastics - women's final

1.00pm: Equestrian - jumping team final

3.00pm: Judo - women's 78kg final and men's 100kg final

6.00pm: Tennis - mixed doubles gold medal match

6.50pm: Trampoline gymnastics - men's final

7.30pm: Swimming - men's 50m freestyle final

7.36pm: Swimming - women's 200m backstroke final

7.43pm: Swimming - men's 200m individual medley final

8.20pm: Athletics - men's 10km final

8.35pm: Cycling (BMX) - men’s final

We use your sign-up to provide content in ways you've consented to and to improve our understanding of you. This may include adverts from us and 3rd parties based on our understanding. You can unsubscribe at any time. Read ourPrivacy Policy

Hungarian boxer Anna Luca Hamori has insisted that she doesn't care about the controversy surrounding her next opponent Imane Khelif.

Khelif won her first bout on Thursday when Angela Carini of Italy quit after just 46 seconds, prompting an outcry on social media.

Hamori, however, is not worried about the prospect of facing the Algerian fighter tomorrow.

"I’m not scared," she said. "I don’t care about the press story and social media. If she or he is a man, it will be a bigger victory for me if I win.

"I’m trying to not use my phone before the fight. I don’t want to care about the comments or the story or the news.

"I just want to stay focused on myself. I did it before my last two fights, so I think this is the key. We will see."



Hello and welcome to Express Sport's rolling coverage of the latest news from the Olympics.

There were no golds for Team GB on Thursday but there will be more opportunities across the action-packed schedule on day seven.

We'll be bringing you the biggest stories from the Games throughout the day, so grab yourself a coffee and stay tuned...

full_text:
Imane Khelif has issued a defiant message to her rivals at theParis 2024 Olympicsafter forcing her opponent to quit just 46 seconds into their fight.

The 25-year-old is competing in the women's 66kg competition and made it through to the quarter-finals following her victory against Italy's Angela Carini.

The bout was shrouded in controversy, however, as Carini decided to quit, reportedly claiming "it's not right" to her corner after Khelif was cleared to compete at the Games.

Khelif was cleared to compete in the women’s boxing category despite being disqualified from last year’s Women’s World Championships before her gold medal bout, after failing to meet the International Boxing Association's (IBA) eligibility criteria.

It was revealed she had failed testosterone and gender eligibility tests, but Khelif was allowed to participate in the women's category at the Olympics and launched into a barrage of punches early on. Carini, whose headgear came unattached, decided to stop the fight on her own terms.

When Khelif was announced as the winner, Carini refused to shake her hand and the Italian could be seen crying in the ring before leaving. Immediately after the fight, the 25-year-old spoke out about her victory.

Khelif refused to stop for long in the interview area after the fight, but the Algerian left with a defiant statement for her critics: "I am here for gold. I’ll fight anyone."

Imane added as she left the ring: "God willing, this was the first victory. God is willing me to be the golden one."

The Algerian Boxing Federation also offered its congratulations to their Olympian on Facebook after the fight and issued a statement condemning perceived “lies” and “unethical targeting and maligning of our esteemed athlete, Imane Khelif, with baseless propaganda from certain foreign media outlets.”



But an emotional Carini was left fearing for her safety after claiming she had never sustained punches like she had during the fight.

"It hurt so much. I am heartbroken. I went to the ring to honour my father," said Carini. "I was told a lot of times that I was a warrior, but I preferred to stop for my health. I have never felt a punch like this.

"After the second blow, and after years of experience in the ring and a lifetime of fighting, I felt extreme pain in my nose."

full_text:
By Mohamed Bahaa

In a brief yet dramatic encounter at the Paris Olympics, Italy's Angela Carini withdrew from her bout against Algeria's Imane Khelif after just 46 seconds. Khelif, who was previously banned from the 2023 World Championships for failing a gender eligibility test, dominated the match, leading Carini to end the fight prematurely.

The Italian boxer signaled her coach, Emanuel Renzini, that she could not continue due to severe pain from Khelifâs punches. Despite Renziniâs encouragement to endure until the round's end, Carini halted the fight again after another punch. "Sheâs too strong," Renzini recalled her saying.

Carini, visibly emotional, apologized to her supporters, expressing regret over the fight's short duration. "I had entered the ring to fight," she said in Italian. "I didn't give up, but a punch hurt too much and so I said enough. I go out with my head held high."

Khelif's dominant performance rekindled debates on whether she and Chinese Taipei's Lin Yu-ting should compete in the Olympics. Both were disqualified from the 2023 World Championships under International Boxing Association (IBA) rules prohibiting athletes with XY chromosomes in women's events. The International Olympic Committee (IOC), however, has taken a more lenient approach, allowing them to compete in Paris, stating, "They are women in their passports."

Mark Adams, IOC spokesperson, defended the decision, highlighting the athletes' long history in the sport. "These athletes competed many times before for many years. They didnât just suddenly arrive."

The IBA, in a statement, maintained their stance on the disqualifications based on thorough reviews and expressed concerns over the IOCâs differing regulations. "The IOCâs differing regulations on these matters, in which IBA is not involved, raise serious questions about both competitive fairness and athletesâ safety," the statement read.

Khelif, who avoided reporters post-fight, received strong support from Algeria's Olympic committee, which condemned foreign media for targeting her. "Such attacks on her personality and dignity are deeply unfair, especially as she prepares for the pinnacle of her career at the Olympics," their statement read.

Italian officials had questioned the fairness of Khelifâs participation prior to the fight. Renzini revealed that many had pressured Carini not to fight, warning of the potential danger. Despite this, Renzini chose a diplomatic stance on Khelif's eligibility. "It's not my decision," he said. "I wouldn't want to be in those shoes because I think it's a very difficult decision."

Khelifâs journey to the Olympics has been marked by significant obstacles. Growing up in rural Algeria, she defied local norms to pursue boxing, traveling long distances and funding her training through scrap metal sales and her mother's couscous business. Her dedication led to notable successes, including a fifth-place finish at the Tokyo Olympics and a silver medal at the 2022 World Championships.

The power behind Khelifâs punches was evident in her bout with Mexicoâs Brianda Tamara in December 2022. Tamara described the fight as the most painful in her 13-year career, even compared to sparring with men.

The debate surrounding Khelifâs eligibility is likely to continue as she advances in the competition. Meanwhile, Carini, who previously paused her career after her father's death before her Tokyo match, faces another Olympic challenge as she prepares for her next bout.

"Itâs not up to me to decide," Carini said, reflecting on the broader implications of her fight against Khelif.

Jessica Hull gave Australia its seventh track and field medal of the Paris Games.Source:AP/Matthias Schrader

Day 14 of the Paris Olympics was painted in silver as Jessica Hull got second place in the 1500m run and the Stingers loss to Spain in the women's water polo final.

The two medals add to the Australian tally, which currently stands at 50: 18 gold, 18 silver and 14 bronze.

Jessica Hull has won Australia's first Olympic middle-distance medal in 56 years in magnificent style, pocketing the silver in three minutes 52.56 seconds - her second-fastest time ever.

She was only denied 1500m gold in Paris by Kenya's Faith Kipyegon, the greatest women's middle-distance runner of all-time.

Kipyegon won in an Olympic record time of 3:51.29, adding a third successive Olympic 1500m title to her name. Britain's Georgia took the bronze.

It was Australia's first Olympic middle-distance medal since Ralph Doubell won 800m gold in 1968 and the first time an Australian woman had pocketed an Olympic medal on the track at a distance further than 800m.

Australia's Stingers have suffered Olympic heartbreak, succumbing in the women's water polo final 11-9 to Spain as their frustrating 24-year wait for the biggest title in the sport goes on.

Unbeaten throughout the tournament, history-making coach Bec Rippon's team finally met their match in a brilliant Spanish side at a throbbing La Defense Arena on Saturday.

Not even five goals from their own brilliant top scorer Alice Williams was enough for the Stingers, who had been attempting to emulate the famous class of Sydney 2000, who won the inaugural women's tournament at home.

Boxer Lin Yu-ting of Taiwan has won Olympic gold in the women's featherweight division following Imane Khelif's lead a day earlier with a glittering response to the intense scrutiny faced by both fighters over misconceptions about their womanhood.

Lin beat Julia Szeremeta of Poland 5-0 in the final at Roland Garros on Saturday night, capping her four-fight unbeaten run through Paris by ensuring Taiwan's first Olympic boxing medal is golden.

On Friday,Khelif won Algeria's first women's boxing medalwith a decisive victory in her own final, beating Yang Liu of China.

Olympic champions Kaylee McKeown and Matt Wearn have been selected as Australia’s closing ceremony flag bearers.

McKeown successfully defended her individual 100m and 200m backstroke Olympic titles in Paris, becoming Australia’s first four-time individual gold medallist, while Wearn won the men’s dinghy sailing event, the first man to win back-to-back titles in this event in Olympic history.

The pair will lead the Australian delegation, expected to include more than 300 athletes from 29 sports set to attend the Closing Ceremony in Stade de France.

Hannah Green, Australia's world No.6 golfer, made a charge at Le Golf National, but just missed out on a medal by one shot, having to settle for a four-way tie for fourth place.

Fijian-born Aussie weightlifter Eileen Cikamatana was left in tears after a disappointing clean-and-jerk ensured she could only finish fourth in the 81kg class when she had been tipped for a medal.

Last year's world diving champ Cassiel Rousseau just missed out on a medal in the 10m platform.

International Olympic Committee president Thomas Bach announced he wouldn't seek to stay in the post at the end of his second term next year, despite calls from members for him to keep going.

matched_title: Olympic Gold Medalist Imane Khelif Files Harassment Claim in Paris
full_text:
BySimon Vozick-Levinson

Three days ago, Algerian boxer Imane Khelifwon a gold medalin the women’s welterweight division of the 2024Olympics. It should have been a triumphant moment for an athlete who spent years training for this moment and won all four of her matches on the world’s biggest stage. Instead, her victory was shadowed by a relentless campaign ofbad-faith criticismfocused on factually baseless claims about hergender. Because the games took place in France, which hasrobust lawsagainst online hate speech, Khelif has now filed a legal complaint with a Paris prosecutor’s office.

According to theAssociated Press, Khelif’s lawyers filed the complaint alleging “aggravated cyber-harassment” on Aug. 9, the day she won the gold medal. The complaint reportedly does not name a specific perpetrator; instead, the prosecutor’s office will conduct an investigation and decide whether to charge anyone under the statute, which could call for two to five years in prison and tens of thousands of Euros in fines.

Attorney Nabil Boudi wrotein the complaintthat the campaign of “speculation fueled by malicious individuals… exceeded 100 million views” on the X platform, becoming an “ordeal” for Khelif.

“I send a message to all the people of the world to uphold the Olympic principles and the Olympic Charter, to refrain from bullying all athletes, because this has effects, massive effects,” Khelif reportedly told the sports video news service SNTV.  She added that online harassment “can destroy people, it can kill people’s thoughts, spirit and mind. It can divide people. And because of that, I ask them to refrain from bullying.”

The manufactured right-wing controversy around Khelif began on Aug. 1, when her opponent, Italian boxer Angela Carini, withdrew from a bout after just 46 seconds, saying, “I have never been hit so hard in my life.” This led to a torrent of specious critiques from people like Elon Musk, J.K. Rowling, Donald Trump, and Logan Paul, all of whom falsely suggested that Khelif is a trans woman and/or that she should be barred from women’s athletic events. In fact, Khelif, 25, was raised as a girl from birth, has never identified as a man, and lives in a country with strict anti-trans laws that would make the scenario invented by conservative critics extremely unlikely. The allegations about her gender are simply not true. (Paul later posteda statementthat began with the word “OOPSIES,” admitting that “I might be guilty of spreading misinformation.”)Trending StoriesJohn Lithgow Knows J.K. Rowling's Anti-Trans Views Will Now Forever Come Up in 'Every Interview'Kevin Spacey’s 'Mental Illness' Should Trigger $29.5 Million Payout, 'House of Cards' Trial Jurors HearBTS Nails Its Comeback With 'Arirang'Top Disaster Response Official Claims He Teleported to a Waffle HouseEditor’s picksThe 250 Greatest Albums of the 21st Century So FarThe 100 Best TV Episodes of All TimeThe 500 Greatest Albums of All Time100 Best Movies of the 21st Century

While the Russian-dominated InternationalBoxingAssociation disqualified Khelif and another boxer from last year’s world championships, that decision has been roundly rejected by the boxing community; the IBA is currently led by a right-wing Putin crony with ahistoryof homophobic and transphobic remarks. International Olympic Committee President Thomas Bach has condemned the“hate speech…aggression and abuse”directed at Khelif, which he linked to Russia’s ongoing conflict with the IOC.

Carini, for her part, swiftly disavowed the entire line of attack being employed by her supposed supporters. The day after the bout, she said she’d like toapologizeto Khelif: “All this controversy certainly made me sad, and I also felt sorry for my opponent, she had nothing to do with it and like me was only here to fight… I have nothing against Khelif and on the contrary if I happened to meet her again I would give her a hug.”

Sport|Olympics

PreviousOlympicgames have been no stranger to controversy and this year’s haven’t been any different.

Fromgenderdebates to questioned victories, here are some of the most talked about moments.

The most recent Olympics controversy involves Great Britain men’s swimmerAdam Peatyhitting out at swimmers “cheating”in the game as he questioned the legitimacy of China’s victory in Sunday’s medley relay.

Great Britain were placed fourth by a Chinese quartet, two of whom tested positive for a banned substance in the lead-up to the Tokyo Olympics. The incident was only brought to light this year in a New York Times investigation.

One of the competitors, Qin Haiyang, also allegedly tested positive for a different performance-enhancing drug several years earlier but he had escaped punishment on contamination grounds.

Following his team’s defeat on Sunday, Peaty called for fairness in the game. He said: “I think you know that truth in your heart. If you touch and you know that you’recheating, you’re not winning, right?

“For me, if you’ve been on that and you have been ‘contaminated’ twice, I think as an honourable person you should be out of the sport.

“I don’t want to paint a whole nation or group of people with one brush, I think that’s very unfair. But there have been two cases of it and it’s very disappointing.”

A 29-year-old beach volleyball player Steven van de Velde was sentenced to four years in prison in 2016 after pleading guilty to raping a 12-year-old girl when he was 19.

The Olympics have drawn criticism following his inclusion in the Netherlands team forParis, with van de Velde staying in a secret location rather than the athletes’ village.

Algerian boxerImane Kheliffound herself in the midst of controversy last Thursday after Italian fighter Angela Cariniabandoned her boutwith Khelif  less than a minute after it started.

Carini, who has since apologised for the level of scrutiny she generated, explained she had never been hit so hard and decided to withdraw because of the intense pain in her nose. The incident amassed millions of views on social media, and triggered responses from celebrities including YouTuber Logan Paul.

Although questions about Khelif’sgendercontinue to swirl online, it has been revealed that Khelif was born a woman and, until last year, had always competed in women’s boxing categories without controversy or attention.

In an interview with SNTV the Algerian athlete said in Arabic: "I send a message to all the people of the world to uphold the Olympic principles and the Olympic Charter, to refrain from bullying all athletes, because this has effects, massive effects.

"It can destroy people, it can kill people's thoughts, spirit and mind. It can divide people. And because of that, I ask them to refrain from bullying."

Triathlontraining in theRiver Seinewas last week cancelled for a second successive day because of the water quality.

It followed growing concerns over the high levels of sewage in the water in the lead-up to theParis Olympics.

A Belgian athlete has since been forced to pull out of the triathlon, after reportedly contracting E.coli.

Belgium have not confirmed whether Michel’s illness is a direct consequence of having swum in the Seine but one Belgian newspaper has reported that the 35-year-old has been too ill to compete in Monday’s mixed triathlon relay.

Sunday’s 100m men’s final left scores of viewers looking at US athlete Noah Lyles, unsure if he had won gold after what was an extremely tight race. Lyles had crossed the finish line next to his closest rival in the shortest time difference since at least Moscow 1980. Lyles finished first at 9.784 seconds compared with Jamaica’s Kishane Thompson who completed the race in 9.789 seconds.

Lyles' decision to lean forward at the end of the race, propelling his chest over the finish line, was the difference in the extremely close call on the purple track at the Stade de France.

Per field and track rules, it is the first athlete whose torso reaches the finish line first is declared the winner.

An Egyptian Olympic volleyball duohave gone viralfollowing a decision to defiantly compete fully clothed in the Olympic Games, despite ahijabban France has imposed on its own athletes.

The announcement had led to huge controversy but one of the players, Doaa Elghobashy, has defended her decision to play with the hijab rather than a bikini like her Spanish opponents.

Following Egypt's 2-0 loss to Spain, the pair were seen embracing, sporting a long-sleeve top, leggings and headscarf.

“I want to play in my hijab, she wants to play in a bikini – everything is okay, if you want to be naked or wear a hijab. Just respect all different cultures and religions,” Elghobashy told Expressen.

“I don't tell you to wear a hijab and you don't tell me to wear a bikini. No one can tell me how to dress.

“It's a free country, everyone should be allowed to do what they want.”

Algerian boxerImane Khalifqualified on Tuesday evening for the final round of the Paris 2024 Olympics Games currently being held in France.

The 33rd edition of the Olympics Games began on July 26, and will end on August 11.

Khelif defeated the Thai champion Janjaem Suwannapheng in the semi-finals of the 66 kg weight in women’s boxing.

The result of the match was 5-0 for the Algerian boxer by unanimous decision of the five referees.

With this result,Khelif qualified for the final round of the ongoing Paris Olympics, officially securing Algeria’s second medal in the Olympics, following Kalia Nemour’s gold in gymnastics.

Khelif faced controversy on her road to the Olympics finals, having been accused of having “male chromosomes”, causing outrage and demands that she be dropped from the Olympics.

In statements made to the Qatari beIN Sports network, the boxer said: “This is a matter of dignity and honor that concerns every woman, and the Arab people have known me for years.”

full_text:
ByOwen Klinsky, Daily Caller News Foundation

August 10, 2024





International Olympic Committee (IOC) President Thomas Bach suggested there is no scientific way to differentiate between men and women during a press conference on Friday.

Sex eligibility rules have been a point ofcontentionat the 2024 Paris Olympics, with Italian boxer Angela Carini withdrawing just 46 seconds into her fight with Algerian fighter Imane Khelif. Perhaps as a result, Bach was asked Friday if the committee would perform a review of its sex change guidelines, in response to which he stated there was no concrete, scientific way to differentiate men from women.

“We have said from the very beginning, if somebody presented us [a] scientifically solid system how to identify men and women we [the IOC] are the first ones to do it,” Bach told reporters. “It is not as easy as some in this cultural world may want to portray it — that the XX or the XY [chromosome] is the clear distinction between men and women.”

As of now, testosterone level — not whether someone has an XX or XY chromosomal pattern — is the key criterion in Olympic eligibility, NBC Newsreported.

Bach also suggested climate change could affect the timing of future Olympics during the Friday press conference,accordingto Sports Illustrated (SI).

“If the climate change is continuing in a way that the experts are forecasting it, then it will be very difficult to organize Olympic Games in summer, in August,” Bach said, according to SI.

The IOC president’s comments come after the 2024 Paris Olympics opening ceremony starred drag queensposingas Christian imagery, including “The Last Supper.”

Norwegian swimmer Henrik Christiansen's viral obsession with the chocolate muffins served in the Olympic Village has captivated global attention. Dubbed the "Olympic Muffin Man," Christiansen's antics have not only entertained but also offered valuable PR lessons for the Olympic organisers.

Christiansen's videos humorously documenting his muffin obsession have become a sensation with tens of millions of views, with Olympics sponsor Adidas even joining in on the fun. His playful content, including declaring himself as the "Olympic Muffin Man" and stashing muffins in a secret drawer, has resonated with audiences, making these muffins a must-try item for athletes and fans alike.

The viral story of Christiansen and his beloved muffins comes at a time when the Olympics have been mired in negative publicity. Recent controversies include the criticism faced by female boxer Imane Khelif, contrasted with the continued participation of convicted rapist Steven van de Velde.

Christiansen's lighthearted muffin mania offers a much-needed positive narrative, showcasing the fun and camaraderie that the Olympics can foster. It provides a refreshing counterpoint to the more contentious issues, generating positive buzz and shifting public focus.

1. Positive distraction:Amid negative press, a lighthearted, viral story can shift public focus and generate positive buzz. The Muffin Man saga has done just that, providing a sweet distraction from more contentious issues.

full_text:
Algerian boxerImane Khelifwill fight for a gold medal on Friday.

The controversial pugilist emerged from her semi-final bout with Thai boxerJanjaem Suwannaphengby unanimous decision in Paris on Tuesday ... making her one of two fighters left standing.

Khelif was welcomed by a mostly supportive crowd ... with some even waving Algeria's green and white flag, and chanting "Imane!"

Of course, Imane's arguably the most polarizing athlete at the entire Olympic Games ... this after questions wereraised about her gender, and whether or not Khelif should be eligible to compete.

The IOC, as well as other officials, have stood firmly by Khelif ... making it clear the fighter has met any/all requirements.

As for Imane, she's admittedly been affected by the controversy ... but she's got an idea of how to respond. Win.

“God willing, this crisis will culminate in a gold medal, and that would be the best response,” the fighter said in an interview conducted in Arabic before being translated into English.

Imane's opponent is not yet known ... that'll be determined on Wednesday in the other semifinal matchup.

What is known -- Khelif's one win away from the gold.

Stay tuned ...

full_text:
Imane Khelifis now under the global spotlight.The Algerian boxer secured her place in the semifinals today. From a small Algerian village, the home of boxer Imane Khelif, her father hailed the athlete as a "heroine."Omar Khelifsaid he raised his daughter "to be brave," while proudly showing a photo of her when she was seven or eight years old, with her hair in braids. "Since she was little, her passion has always been sports," said the 49-year-old, sitting with two of his younger children. On Thursday, whenAngela Carinileft the ring just 46 seconds into the match, his daughter Imane found herself at the center of a global controversy with insinuations about her sexual identity.

According to theIOC, there is no indication that Khelif, who has fought in the women's circuit for years, including theTokyoOlympics, identifies as anything other than a woman. Her father showed identity documents and her birth certificate to AFP, speaking from a rural village about 10 km from Tiaret, a city 300 km fromAlgiers. "My daughter is a girl," said Omar Khelif. "She was raised as a girl. She is a strong girl; I raised her to work and be brave. She won against the Italian simply because she was stronger, and the other was weaker."

Imane has a "strong will to work and train," assured her father. "IfGodwills, she will honor us with a gold medal and raise our national flag inParis. This is the only goal we have set from the beginning."

matched_title: Israel Adesanya apologizes to controversial Olympian Imane Khelif over recent transgender allegations: "My bad!" | BJPenn.com
full_text:
Home»MMA News»Israel Adesanya»Israel Adesanya apologizes to controversial Olympian Imane Khelif…

Former UFC middleweight champion Israel Adesanya has apologized to controversial Olympian Imane Khelif after labeling her as a biological man.

Adesanya returns at UFC 305 later this month against UFC middleweight titleholder Dricus du Plessis in Perth. He returns to the Octagon after almost a full year away from fighting, stemming from a loss to Sean Strickland at UFC 293.

Adesanya took some heat last week onsocial mediawhen he publically attacked Khelif and accused her of fighting women as a biological man. Khelif failed a gender eligibility test at a 2023 event, stoking the flames of speculation regarding her 2024 Summer Olympics appearance in women’s boxing.

After it was reported that Khelif suffers from Swyer syndrome, a rare condition in which women can have an X and a Y chromosome (man), Adesanya has backtracked on his stance.

During a recent interview withThe Rock, a radio station in New Zealand, Adesanya apologized to Khelif.

“From now on, I will leave women’s fights to women. I tried to speak up about it and apparently, I got it wrong, a lot of people got it wrong, there was misinformation,” Adesanya said. “I spoke up about it because I don’t like to get triggered, but when something triggers me, I’m a fighter and I saw that I’m like, ‘What?’ And I saw the news or the articles speaking on it, ‘Why they letting a trans person [in]?’ Then I find out later, okay, she wasn’t actually trans…

“So, to the lady, my bad. I got that wrong. But for me, still is that — all this grey area, even now, they let some pedo guy compete. Wait, so what? He served his time, cool, but you’re still going to let him compete in the Olympics and that goes against the guidelines of the Olympics’ rules … anyway, to the lady, the boxer, I am sorry. I didn’t understand what was going on.” (h/tMMA Mania/Drake Riggs)

As of this writing, Khelif hasn’t responded nor acknowledged Adesanya’s comments. She recently called for the“bullying”against her to end following a tumultuous week at the 2024 Summer Olympics.

matched_title: La boxe féminine dans la tourmente olympique : qui est l'Algérienne Imane Khelif et quels sont les problèmes auxquels elle est confrontée ?
full_text:
Professor of Sociology of Sport, Nord University

Anne Tjønndal est affiliée à la Fédération norvégienne de boxe.

View all partners

https://doi.org/10.64628/AAJ.q35djf57s

Share article

Print article

L'Algérienne Imane Khelif est l'une des deux boxeuses des Jeux olympiques de Paris 2024 à fairel'actualité- alors queles controversesautour des tests de genre et destaux de testostéronerefont surface. Cette polémique rappelle lecasde l'athlète sud-africaine Caster Semenya. Khelif et la boxeuse taïwanaise Lin Yu-ting ont échoué à untest de genre douteuxadministré par l'Association internationale de boxe amateur (IBA) en 2023, mais ont été déclarées aptes à participer aux Jeux par le Comité international olympique. Toutes deux sont des femmes et s'identifient comme telles, maisl'une des adversairesde Khelif a suscité un tollé en affirmant qu'elle bénéficiait d'un avantage injuste et qu'elle était transgenre. Nous avons sollicité l'avis de Anne Tjønndal,sociologue du sportet ancienne boxeuse, pour nous éclairer sur ce point.

La boxe a été l'un des derniers sports olympiques d'été à accueillir des femmes athlètes. La boxe féminine a été introduite dans le programme olympique lors des Jeux de Londres 2012. À Londres,seulement troisdes dix catégories de poids ont été incluses pour les femmes : 51 kg (poids mouche), 60 kg (poids léger) et 75 kg (poids moyen). Lors de ces mêmes jeux, dix catégories de poids étaient prévues pour les boxeurs masculins. La marginalisation de la boxe féminine s'est poursuivie tout au long des Jeux de Rio 2016, avec le maintien de trois catégories de poids pour les femmes et de dix catégories de poids pour les hommes. Les choses ont commencé à changer après Rio, les Jeux de Tokyo 2020 incluant cinq catégories de poids pour les femmes et huit catégories pour les hommes. À Paris, les hommes concourent dans sept catégories de poids et les femmes dans six.

L'une des plus grandes controverses sur la boxe féminine aux Jeux olympiques a eu lieu avant Londres 2012, lorsque l'AIBA (Association internationale de boxe amateur, aujourd'hui l'IBA) a proposé que les boxeuses soient obligées de porter une jupe pendant les compétitions. Cette proposition a étérejetéepar les boxeuses du monde entier. Bien que la question soit restée en suspens jusqu'enjanvier 2012, il a finalement été annoncé au printemps 2012 que les boxeuses seraient autorisées à choisir de concourir en jupe ou en short.

Aujourd'hui, les principaux enjeux sociaux pour la boxe féminine aux Jeux olympiques portent sur la sous-représentation des femmes dans les rôles d'entraîneur et de leadership, ainsi que sur le manque de recherche concernant la prévalence du harcèlement et des abus sexuels dans le milieu de la boxe.

Imane Khelif est une boxeuse amateur algérienne. Elle a remporté une médaille d'argent au championnat du monde 2022 et une médaille d'or au championnat d'Afrique 2022. AuxJeux olympiques de Tokyo 2020, elle a atteint les quarts de finale. Sonpalmarès amateurcompte 51 combats (39 victoires et 9 défaites) au moment de la rédaction de cet article.

Dans l'actualité récente, Khelif a été victime d'une désinformation généralisée et d'allégations infondées concernant son sexe. Elle a été accusée à tort d'être transgenre, alors qu'elle a vécutoute sa vieen tant que femme et qu'elle mène depuis longtemps une carrière dans la boxe féminine. La controverse est née de la disqualification de Khelif par l'IBA pour le championnat du monde de 2023. L'IBA a invoqué le fait qu'elle n'avait pas satisfait auxtests d'éligibilité de genre, mais n'a pas précisé la nature de ces tests, ce qui a suscité des rumeurs selon lesquelles Khelif est transgenre et biologiquement de sexe masculin.

Read more:Misinformation, abuse and injustice: breaking down the Olympic boxing firestorm

La situation s'est aggravée après que Khelif a remporté son premier combat olympique en 46 secondes contre l'Italienne Angela Carini. Cette victoire a entraîné une recrudescence des abus sur les réseaux sociaux à l'encontre de Khelif. Le commentaire de Carini après le combat, affirmant qu'elle n'avait jamais étéfrappée aussi fortauparavant, a alimenté les allégations. Bien que Carini se soit par la suiteexcuséeauprès de Khelif pour son comportement, la controverse a été exacerbée lorsque l'IBA a annoncé que Carini recevrait un prix équivalent à celui des médaillés d'or. La Fédération italienne de boxe a depuisdéclaréqu'elle refusera un tel prix.

La plupart des compétitions sportives sont divisées en catégories de sexe : une pour les femmes et une pour les hommes. Cette séparation est un principe fondamental visant à assurer l'équité dans les sports d'élite, en raison des différences physiques et athlétiques entre les sexes.

Read more:Caster Semenya: the legal and ethical issues that should concern us all

Les tests de genre sont utilisés dans lescompétitions sportives de haut niveau depuis les années 1920, leur objectif initial étant d'empêcher les hommes de se faire passer pour des femmes afin de participer aux sports féminins.

Cependant, au fil des décennies, les “marqueurs sexuels” testés ont évolué avec les progrès des connaissances médicales. Plusieurs de ces tests se sont révélés médicalementinexacts et éthiquement problématiques.).

Le test de testostérone est actuellement fréquemment utilisé dans les procédures de détermination du genre. Les femmes athlètes ayant des niveaux de testostérone jugés “trop élevés” peuvent se voir demander de réguler artificiellement leur taux, même si leur corps produit naturellement plus de testostérone que celui d'autres femmes athlètes. Cette exigence concerne principalement deux groupes : les femmes transgenres et les femmes présentant des variations intersexuelles (différences de développement sexuel ou DSD). À l'heure actuelle, il n'y a aucune indication que Khelif ou Yu-ting soient affectées par un DSD.

L'Association internationale de boxe (IBA) était auparavant l'organisme dirigeant la boxe amateur (olympique). Cependant, le Comité international olympique (CIO) luia retirécette prérogative en raison de problèmes persistants de corruption et de gouvernance. Pour les Jeux olympiques de Paris, une organisation désignée par le CIO est chargée de l'organisation des compétitions de boxe. Pour les Jeux de 2028, une nouvelle entité appelée World Boxing devrait remplacer l'IBA en tant qu'organe dirigeant de la boxe olympique.

Read more:Olympics: Namibia's sprinters highlight a flawed testosterone testing system

Les conflits de pouvoir et les tensions entre l'IBA et le CIO ont suscité des interrogations sur les véritables motivations de l'IBA concernant les questions d'éligibilité des sexes dans la boxe olympique. Beaucoup estiment que l'IBA instrumentalise la situation de Khelif pour affaiblir le CIO et gagner un soutien international.

Historiquement, les femmes ont été marginalisées dans la boxe, que ce soit en tant qu'athlètes, entraîneuses, arbitres ou dirigeantes. La boxe reste majoritairement dominée par les hommes dans de nombreux pays, ce qui restreint souvent les opportunités pour les femmes. Actuellement, le manque de représentation féminine dans les rôles d'entraîneurs et de dirigeants constitue un problème majeur dans la boxe féminine.

En matière de critères d'éligibilité de genre dans les sports de haut niveau, les femmes provenant du Sud global sontsurreprésentéesdans les situations où le genre des femmes athlètes est mis en doute.

Murray has now played his last match as a professional tennis player, with tributes starting to pour in.

Speaking after his defeat, the 37-year-old admitted that he'd initially been reluctant to walk away from tennis.

Andy Murray's time as a tennis player is over following his defeat at the Olympics

PA

But Murray's stance has now changed, with the three-time Grand Slam winner at peace with his decision to hang up his racket.

“A few months ago I was not looking forward to it," he told Eurosport.

"Like in most jobs when people retire generally it’s more of a celebration because they’re looking forward to it.

“I’ve obviously loved playing tennis so I wasn’t necessarily really looking forward to that moment.

"But after I had the operation on my back at Queen’s, I was struggling to walk. It was kind of an emergency operation that I had to have.

“I’ve been looking forward to the end [since then] because physically it’s been tough and I’m not able to move around the court and do the things I want to do physically.

“Therefore I can’t compete to the level that I want to.

"So yeah I’m happy to be finishing now. I was worried about it in the last year or 18 months, what I’d do with myself, but I’m looking forward to the end.”

READ MORE:Tom Daley makes complaint about Olympic Village as diving star talks Andy Murray and Rafael Nadal encounters

Murray had been hoping to win a third Olympic gold medal.

He won his first back in 2012, beating Roger Federer just weeks after losing to the Swiss icon in the final of Wimbledon.

And Murray got his second at the Rio Olympics in 2016, with the tennis star beating Juan Martin del Potro.

Murray competed in arguably the hardest era of tennis due to having to compete against the likes of Federer, Rafael Nadal and Novak Djokovic.

PA

But he's proud of everything he's achieved in the sport, with the veteran adding: "I'm proud of my career, my achievements and what I put into the sport.

"Obviously it was emotional because it's the last time I will play a competitive match.

"But I am genuinely happy just now. I'm happy with how it finished."

It is currently unclear what Murray will do next.

He's previously confessed he's not the biggest fan of punditry, with coaching a more realistic prospect.

Murray will, for now, bask in the glory of his career. With three Grand Slams and two gold medals, he's arguably the finest sportsperson Britain has ever seen.

Well, perhaps, these last two weeks have taught us to forget all those stereotypes about the French, with their smiling waiters, reliable public transport and cooling rain.

And as for their great summer tradition of strikes and riots, unfortunately that moved a little closer to home.

Whisper it quietly but Paris saw London 2012 and raised it, bringing theOlympicsto the heart of the world’s most Instagrammable city. Wherever you hopped off the Metro a viral moment was just a short walk away.

Their opening ceremony left a little to be desired. You had to respect the ambition but four hours of interpretive dance, staged in pouring rain, couldn’t even be saved by a spectacular light show and Celine Dion.

But after the storm, came the sun, the sport and Snoop Dogg. They say you’re never far from the Eiffel Tower in Paris and Snoop lurked everywhere too.

Their closing night show played it a little more safe and a touch too melancholy, Olympic officials making it privately clear there were unhappy with some ofthe more risque elements of Thomas Jolly’s sodden opening night.

Athletes were honoured, volunteers were thanked, President Macron even got a small cheer, though if Leon Marchand wants to run for office, he’d win in a landslide, helping extinguish the flame at the Jardin des Tuileries.

Paris, that city of die-hard romantics, seems a rather fitting place to fall back in love with the Olympics, 12 years after London wrote their blueprint for success.

They’ve been relentlessly worrying about this fortnight here for seven years but they’ve been just what Macron needed, a temporary truce on the political divisiveness that remains his most pressing problem.

Jacques Chirac’s popularity peaked after France won the World Cup, Macron’s motorcade often swept into venues from the Elysees Palace just moments before another gold was won.

The reported cost of this two-week circus, remember the Paralympics are still to come, was £7.8 billion, the ‘cheapest’ summer Games in two decades. The biggest single cost was security, you were never far from a gendarmerie with a gun.

As ever the challenges comes with what’s next. Team GB - fuelled by crucial National Lottery investment - bucked a trend by winning more medals in the Games post-hosting in Rio but that’s no easy task for the French, who enjoyed their best medal return for 124 years.

These Games have been bon if not magnifique for Great Britain - only the United States and China won more medals than Team GB’s 65, though their 14 golds put them seventh on the medal table, outside their stated two targets - top five and top European nation.

“The Games isn’t just about medal success,” said triathlon gold medallist Alex Yee, who carried the British flag with gymnast Bryony Page at the closing ceremony. “If we’re achieving the same medals as London, I think that’s a hugely successful Games.”

Medals or moments, what comes first? For those who fund our sport then their millions of public money demands a return on investment measured in gold, silver and bronze.

But no-one watching at home is losing any sleep that Team GB got a few less gold medals than Australia, France or the Netherlands.

While some headlines will say this is their most disappointing return in 20 years, the facts behind the stats are not so binary.

Yes all that glittered was bronze, 29 third places was the most since 1908, when the Games started in April and finished in October.

But in total 40% of the team came medallists, more than Tokyo and 18 different sports contributed British podium spots.

We’ve become a bit too obsessed with golds, perhaps forgetting it’s just 28 years since Team GB returned from Atlanta with just one.

Remember these Games and it won’t just be golds but Andy Murray and Dan Evans’s improbable comeback in saving five match points, Amber Rutter’s dramatic penalty shoot-out for silver,Josh Kerr’s race for the ages with Cole Hockerand that Norwegian guy who talked quicker than he ran.

And then there’s Cindy Ngamba -still fighting for her British citizenship- winning the Refugee Team’s first medal with boxing bronze.

Some found themselves in the headlines for other reasons, would Imane Khelif swap the fortnight of bile she has endured for the gold she wore asshe proudly carried the Algerian flag into the opening ceremony?

Would Australian breakdancer B-Girl Raygun rather have not become an Olympian if she knew the trolling she’d be on the end of for achieving something that 99.9% of the world can only dream about?

Besides, modern Olympic founder Pierre de Courbetin hated medal tables, a media invention that was banned by the Olympic Charter - and ignored by all - until just a few years ago.

The Olympics are a perfect ideal that are far from perfect and their guardians must reflect on their decision to allow convicted child rapist Steven van de Velde to compete and admit some accountability for the appalling treatment of boxer Khelif too.

But the show rolls on, from the City of Lights to the City of Angels.

The handover ceremony made it clear that Los Angeles will be big, brash and super-sized, these Games of chic and elan couldn’t be more different but are no easy act to follow.

Editorial: The Games helped bring the world together when it seems to be at its most divided in decades

Removed from bookmarks

The Paris Olympics got off to arather shaky, wet,rat-infestedand sabotaged start, passed through more than a fortnight of gripping sporting excitement, and culminated in the unalloyed relief that the event passed without any of the potential civil unrest or terrorism that might have turned joy into tragedy. Only the lone individualclimbing the Eiffel Towerin the last hours of the proceedings came close to any sense of jeopardy.

Yes, it was a shame that the Seine couldn’t be cleaned up in time, but Paris is not the only capital city in the world withpolluted waterways. Aside from some malicious disruption of rail and other communications, Paris was spared the crimes and scandals of previous Games. It is something to be grateful for in a world seemingly trapped incyclesofwarandextremism.

Indeed, it may be said that the last few weeks have seen the French capital become a beacon of hope intorrid times. What we saw athletes from all over the world display was that spirit of intense but peaceful competition that was so much a part of the ancient as well as the modern event.

The antagonism of one competitor against another, to the ancient Greeks, implied strife; while competition more as a simple struggle to come first was a virtue. And, with some rare exceptions, that same spirit infused those taking part in Paris – fellow athletes embracing one another and celebrating together in mutual respect in a supremely multicultural, multiethnic environment.

There were many memorable moments, and most for all the right reasons. One which particularly stood out – and which went especially viral – was the display put on by the Australian “Raygun” (real name Rachael Gunn), who recalled Great Britain’s plucky skier Eddie “the Eagle” Edwards, the sensation of the 1988 Winter Olympics, with her endearingly eclectic breakdancing performance.

These days, thanks to the National Lottery, Team GB can look forward to every set of Olympics with the justified expectation of a disproportionately large haul of medals, and Paris has been no exception. True, the performance hasn’t been quite as strong as at some other recent championships, but the merits of the then prime minister John Major’s idea of a national lottery as a way to bypass the difficult politics of funding sport through general taxation have become glitteringly clear. It may even turn out to be the abiding success of his premiership.

FromKeely Hodgkinson in the women’s 800m, toKaty Marchant, Emma Finucane and Sophie Capewellin track cycling, toToby Robertsin the climbing, toEmma Hayes leading the US women’s football team to gold, there were plenty of outstanding, world-beating achievements.

So, Paris was one of the more successful Games, though far from free of controversy. The gender status of the Algerian boxer Imane Khelif became arenewed focus for the debate on trans peoplein sport (she isnot trans, as it happens), and, all too predictably, was traduced during one of Donald Trump’s tedious, rambling US presidential rallies. Suffice to say, the rows about Khelif confirm how much more work needs to be done to achieve a consensus on chromosomes in sport.

More broadly, the Games brought the world together. They are one of the few events of any kind that are genuinely global, and in troubled times, the modern Olympics serve two complementary roles – distraction and inspiration.

It is no bad thing to gain some respite from stories of war and political strife, and the athletes can always inspire others to emulate their accomplishments in sports and other walks of life. A figure such as Simone Biles, whose four medals in Paris have taken her overall tally to 11, redeemed herself in gymnastics after suffering a mental block, “the twisties” at Tokyo three years ago – encompassing an important message about mental health.

As the torch is passed once again, we look forward to the “Hollywood Olympics”, in Los Angeles in 2028, and hope they will pass on more peaceful, happier times (and withoutMr Trumppresiding over them with a running commentary).

title_norm: the most memorable and controversial moments of the paris olympics 2024 metro news
matched_title: The most memorable and controversial moments of the Paris Olympics 2024 | Metro News
full_text:
The curtain has finally come down on a spectacularOlympic GamesinParis.

The French capital and its host of landmarks provided the perfect backdrop for events across the city during 16 days of sporting action.

Great Britain departed with an impressive hail of 65 medals (14 gold, 22 silver and 29 bronze), good enough for seventh in the medal table withUnited Statestopping the table ahead ofChina.

After an exhilarating Games filled with memories, controversy, triumph and heartbreak, we say ‘au revoir’ and look back on the moments that captivated and stirred controversy in the French capital.

Adam Peatywas one of Team GB’s early gold medal favourites but failed to defend his 100m breaststroke title – losing out to Italy’s Nicolo Martinenghiby an unbelievably narrow margin of 0.02 seconds.

Peaty was comforted by his family in the immediate aftermath, including his three-year-old son George, with the Olympian revealing the touching moment that left him – and us – in tears.

‘George normally goes, “Daddy, are you the fastest boy?”, and I would’ve said, “Not today”,’ Peaty explained.

‘But he says, “I love you, Daddy”, and that’s all I care about.’

After the despair of Tokyo three years ago, absolutely everyone was rooting forSimone Bilesto return to her sublime best this summer. She delivered.

Biles left Paris with three golds and a silver medalwith a series of stunning displays in her gymnastics events.

Celebrities flocked to watch Biles in action and after her Tokyo campaign was derailed by the ‘twisties’, she sealed the perfect redemption story.

Anthony Ammirati became a Paris legend without winning a medal as the video of the well-endowed pole vaulter went viral.

Roared on by a home crowd, Ammirati’s hopes of clinching a medal were deniedafter his bulge sent the bar flying while attempting to clear the 5.70m stageof his event.

While it was a moment of sporting heartbreak for him personally, Ammirati has thankfully seen the funny side, becoming a social media sensation in the process.

When it was confirmed the Olympics would beAndy Murray’s last hurrah, there was a feeling he wouldn’t be going quietly.

While there was no medal at the end of it, the Scot bowed out in typically exhilarating fashion alongside doubles partner Dan Evans, thrilling the crowdwith two dramatic victories at Roland Garros before a quarter-final exit.

While there was no fairytale ending, it was very much a departure on his terms as the British sporting legend said goodbye on the grandest stage.

Many who casually tuned into the air pistol shooting one Wednesday afternoonwere left convinced Turkey had ‘sent a hitman’ as part of their medal hunt, such was the poise and style of Yusuf Dikec.

Typically, pistol shooters compete wearing specialist eyewear to help improve accuracy and huge protectors in order to block out any distractions. Not Yusef, however.

Wearing his regular prescription glasses and a standard pair of earplugs, he effortlessly shot his way to silver as Olympic fans celebrated a new hero.

Just as the Olympic Games were drawing to a close, Australian breakdancer Raygun provided us with one more unforgettable moment.

Her routine stunned and delighted viewers in almost equal measure,but drew no love from event judges who did not reward her a single point.

‘Don’t be afraid to be different,’ she defiantly said on social media. ‘Go out there and represent yourself, you never know where that’s gonna take you.’

Perhaps the biggest story from the Paris Games surrounded Algeria’s Imane Khelif, whocelebrated a superb gold medal success on Friday night in her boxing event.

But her participation in the French capital over the last two weeks has been under intense scrutiny after she was disqualified from last year’s Women’s World Championships for allegedly failing International Boxing Association (IBA) gender eligibility rules.

The International Olympic Committee cleared Khelif to fight and has backed her inclusion throughout the Games.

The row intensified during Khelif’s fight against Angela Carini when the Italian quit after just 46 seconds, explaining post-fight she had ‘been hit so hard she couldn’t breathe.’ Carini later apologised to Khelif.

The filthy River Seine was a problem from the opening days of the Gameswith the triathlon postponed over pollution levels in the water– not ideal when you have a 1.5km swim ahead of you.

Triathletes were seen violently vomiting in the aftermath of what is already a torturous event. Belgium pulled its team from the mixed relay event after an athlete who previously swam in the river got sick, and a swimmer for Switzerland also missed the competition due to illness.

Millions were spent trying to clean the iconic stretch of water and French authorities were adamant there were no issues over the safety of the river with Paris mayor Anne Hidalgo even taking a dip to ease any concerns. But not everyone was convinced.

Cases of athletes being sent home for alleged bad behaviour was a theme throughout the Games.

Brazil’s Carolina Vieira was kicked out of the Olympics Villagefor allegedly sneaking off the premises with her boyfriend – later revealing she didn’t even get the chance to pack her bags.

Paraguay’sLuana Alonso was also removed from the village for ‘inappropriate conduct’shortly after missing out on a semi-final place in her swimming event. Reports also suggested Alonso snuck off the premises to squeeze in a quick trip to Disneyland rather than supporting her teammates.

Fresh after winning 100m, Noah Lyles returned to the track for the 200m finals two days later but appeared to be in some distress during the latter stages of that longer sprint, settling for bronze.

Moments after the athletes crossed the finish line, it emerged Lyles had tested positive for Covid ahead of the race and was later pictured being taken off the track in a wheelchair in the French capital.

Lyles revealed he had been feeling ‘really horrible’ two days before the final, leading many to question his decision to compete given the risks over mixing with fellow athletes in the lead-up.

BBC’s Gabby Logan described it as a ‘selfish decision’ with Michael Johnson ‘morally unsure’ over his participation – with Lyles also seen embracing teammate Kenny Bednarek who would later compete in the 4X100 relay race.

full_text:
The medals flowed on Day 11 of theParis2024OlympicsasTeam GBfollowedKeely Hodgkinson’s leadwith yet more success.

On four wheels, 16-year-old sensation Sky Brown brought home a second bronze in the women’s skateboarding, the same medal she won on her Olympic debut in Tokyo three years ago.

Ben Maher looked to defend his Olympic title in the individual jumping on his horse, Point Break, but it wasn’t to be for the 41-year-old, who missed out on the medals altogether.

Andrea Spendolini-Sirieix qualified in third for the 10m diving final, though wasunable to clinch another medalfollowing herbronze in the synchro.

In the velodrome British trio of Ed Lowe, Hamish Turnbull and Jack Carlin had to settle for the runners-up prize in the men’s team sprint, with the Netherlands setting two world records to clinch gold.

And it was silver again for Team GB later in the evening – this time on the track – as Josh Kerr was pipped to gold by America’s Cole Hocker after a pulsating men’s 1,500m race.

In the ring, GB’s Lewis Richardson had to settle for bronzeafter a controversial split-decision defeat,while Algeria’s Imane Khelif booked her place in the women’s 66kg final.

This blog is now closed. For the latest updates, visitMetro’s Sport page.

That’s all for today at the Paris Olympics and it’s been another enthralling day of action. We’ll be back at the same time tomorrow morning to keep you up to date with all the action.

Goodnight!

The action keeps coming tomorrow, with more medal hopes for Team GB on the track, in the velederome and elsewhere.

Here’s just some of what we’re set up for – make sure to set your alarms!

Athletics

Basketball

Beach volleyball

Boxing

Cycling (track)

Diving

Golf

Hockey

Sailing

Skateboarding

Sport climbing

Weightlifting

Here is how the medal table looks as Day 11 in Paris draws to a close. A trio of late gold medals move the USA ahead of China and into first place, while GB move closer to France having picked up two silvers and two bronzes.

It’s looking tight in that battle for third place.

Team GBboxer Lewis Richardson admitted he was left ‘devasted’ by his controversial semi-final defeat to Marco Verde in the ParisOlympic Games.

Here what the welterweight boxer had to say after he was forced to settle for a bronze medal in Paris:

Team GB star Lina Nielsen broke down in tears and opened up on her battle with multiple sclerosis after she fell in the semi-finals of the 400m women’s hurdles at theParisOlympics.

Nielsen had ambitions of making the final but the 28-year-old’s dreams went up in smoke as she clattered into the final barrier of the opening semi-final.

And after the race, the Brit was consoled by a reporter as she opened up on her difficult journey just to make it to Paris:

Kellie Harrington is overcome with emotion as the Irish fighter retains her 60kg Olympic title with a convincing display over old rival Yang Wenlu.

With that win – her last amateur bout – the 34-year-old becomes the first-ever Irish boxer to win gold in two consecutive Olympic Games and she celebrates wildly with her team and the hundreds of Irish fans crammed into the stadium here in Paris.

A great moment and the impressive summer Games continue for Ireland.

A great win against the reigning World champions as Brazil beat Spain 4-2 to book their place in the final of the women’s football.

They will take on USA, now managed by former Chelsea manager Emma Hayes, in Saturday’s final.

And it’s a dominant display from the number five seed Khelif who books her spot in the final with a unanimous decision win over Janjaem Suwannapheng.

The Algerian won both of the opening rounds by unanimous decision and made no mistake in the final three minutes, even forcing a standing count after a great combination.

It’s a great atmosphere at Court Philippe Chatrier as the Parisian crowd cheers on the victorious Khelif who will now go for gold later in the week as she bids to win her nation’s first boxing gold.

The Algerian boxer has endured a controversial road to this point but is now three rounds away from the gold medal bout in the women’s 66kg division.

Thai fighter Janjaem Suwannapheng is standing in her way.

An impressive display from GB’s Jacob Fincham-Dukes who comes up just short of the medals with a leap of 8.14m.

But it’s gold for Greece Miltiadis Tentoglou who defends his Olympic title with a mark of 8.48. Jamaica’s Wayne Pinnock came second with 8.36m and Italy’s Mattia Furlani took bronze with 8.34m.

GB’s Lewis Richardson thinks he has done enough but the welterweight comes up just short in his semi-final bout against Mexico’s Marco Verde.

A split-decision defeat means he has to settle for bronze and that will be Britain’s only medal in the ring at Paris this Games.

Verde, meanwhile, will now fight Uzbekistan’s Asadkhuja Muydinkhujaev for the gold medal.

It was a pulsating men’s 1,500m final but GB’s Josh Kerr was forced to settle for silver as America’s Cole Hocker claimed a shock win in Paris.

Former Olympic Champion Michael Johnson provided his analysis of the race:

American’s Gabby Thomas was the favourite and she made no mistake, pipping 100m winner Julien Alfred to take home the gold.

Brittany Brown takes bronze but it’s heartache for Team GB with Dina Asher-Smith and Daryll Neita coming fourth and fifth respectively.

And there’s plenty of interest from a Team GB perspective, with Dina Asher-Smith and Daryll Neita both involved.

Julien Alfred will look to do the double having won 100m gold earlier in the Games.

An Olympic record for Bahrain’s Yavi as she pips defending Olympic champion Peruth Chemutai on the line. Bronze for Kenya’s Faith Cherotich.

It’s a great run from GB’s Elizabeth Bird, too, who crosses the line in seventh but sets a new national record in the process.

‘I’m so proud of myself, I executed the best 1500m race of my life on the biggest stage by over a second,’ the Brit says speaking on Eurosport.

‘It’s difficult to control other people but I positioned myself to win and I did that. It wasn’t enough over the last 30m but I can walk away with my head held high.

‘All I can do is be focusing on my race. If you start worrying about the race and other peoples’ work you run a fine line of being disappointed everywhere.

‘I wanted gold, I got silver, it’s better than bronze – we’re moving in the right direction.’

A pulsating race all the way up to the finish line…

WOW!! ð¤¯USA’s Cole Hocker just shredded the script! He’s taken gold with GB’s Josh Kerr in silver. Jakob Ingebrigtsen dropped to fourth!#BBCOlympics#Olympics#Paris2024pic.twitter.com/TgAigLmcD0

Sebastian Coe billed tonight’s 1500m final as a ‘race for the ages’ between arch-rivals Josh Kerr and Jakob Ingebrigtsen – and it did not disappoint.

But there’s a huge shock in Paris as Cole Hocker wins gold for the USA!

Ingebrigtsen stormed to the front in a rapid start to the race and the only question was whether or not the Norwegian would be able to sustain the pace. The answer: no he couldn’t.

In a pulsating finish on the home straight, Kerr overtook Ingebrigtsen but was passed himself by Hocker, who held firm to secure a famous victory.

In the end, Ingebrigtsen crossed the finish line in fourth, with USA’s Yared Nuguse clinching bronze at the death.

What. A. Race.

Team GB take silver after a historic men’s team sprint final!

Ed Lowe, Hamish Turnbull and Jack Carlin knew they were in for an almighty battle against the Netherlands and it wasn’t to be for the British trio, who had to settle for the runners-up prize.

The Dutch outfit opened up a sizeable gap with one lap remaining of the velodrome and stormed home in 40.949 seconds to set a second world record in as many rides.

Different class from the Dutch.

Matthew Hudson-Smith is into the men’s 400m final â#BBCOlympics#Paris2024#Olympicspic.twitter.com/1JJG3rvm9e

It doesn’t get much more emphatic than this…

Team GB’s Matthew Hudson-Smith books his place in the men’s 400m final with a thunderous time of 44.07 seconds in his semi-final.

European silver medallist Charlie Dobson finished fifth in his heat and will not be competing in tomorrow’s showpiece at the Stade de France.

matched_title: Why USA think they're top of the Olympics medal table - when they're not | Metro News
full_text:
TheUnited Statesof America are the favourites to win theParis2024Olympics, no surprise given they are the most successful nation in the games’ history.

Prior to the start of this summer’s games, aSuperComputer predictedthat Team USA would win a whopping 121 medals including 39 gold, far more than nearest rivals China and Team GB.

At the time of writing on day six they’ve made a great start winning 31 medals, including six golds to put them way out in front in the medal table – at least that’s what US media would tell you.

The reality is they are currently only fifth in the table, behind China, France, Japan and Australia.Great Britain are narrowly behind in sixth.

Follow Metro.co.uk’s live blog for updates from the 2024 Paris Olympics

So why the confusion? Well the International Olympic Committee ranks nations by thenumber of gold medals they’ve won, followed by the number of silver won, and then bronze.

It’s a convention that virtually every nation follows, but the USA are a notable exception. Instead, their ranking is based on thetotal number of medals won,regardless of their colour.

As a result, many US media outlets are reporting that they are easily winning in Paris, with their total of 31 medals putting them clear of second-placed France on 26.

But the rest of the world has them fifth, having won five fewer golds than their main rivals China.

The use of different medal tables has caused uproar at past games in both the summer and winter Olympics, notably at the 2008 games in Beijing.

On that occasion, China won with 48 golds to the USA’s 36, but the latter won 112 medals in total compared to the host’s 100.

Of course, the USA are expected to top both medal tables, having so far won gold in the swimming, rowing, fencing and gymnastics courtesy of their brilliant women’s team headlined by Simone Biles.

title_norm: steven van de veldes partner pleads with olympic crowds after boos for rapist metro news
matched_title: Steven van de Velde's partner pleads with Olympic crowds after boos for rapist | Metro News
full_text:
Steven van de Velde’s partner says he was ‘disappointed’ with the crowd reaction after the convicted rapist was relentlessly booed again at theOlympic GamesinParis.

Van de Velde was sentenced to four years in prison in 2016after pleading guilty to raping a 12-year-old British girl when he was 19.

He resumed his volleyball career after serving 12 months of a four-year sentence and was named in the Netherlands’ Olympic squad in June.

Follow Metro.co.uk’s live blog for updates from the Paris 2024 Olympics

The 29-year-old wasbooed during his first appearance at the beach volleyball event on Sundayand was given an even more hostile reception as the Dutch took on Chile on Wednesday afternoon.

Van de Velde was booed every time he served at the Eiffel Tower Stadium with his volleyball partner Matthew Immers again defending his teammate.

‘I don’t want to say much about it. What was in the past is in the past,’ he told reporters. ‘I can’t change his past. I’m here to play with him. We want to have good results here. There are a lot of people who support us. We do it for them.

‘He has a lot of pressure on his shoulders. I’m OK with that. Mentally we are strong, I am strong, we will get through this together.’

On the increased booing van de Velde received, Immers added: ‘It is what it is. I was disappointed with the fans. We talked on the field and asked ourselves: what do we need from each other? It was worse than in the first game. We need each other on the field, the hugs, the cheering.’

Van de Velde’s inclusion in the Dutch squad has been persistently defendedby teammates and the team’s selectors and decision-makers.

‘Steven has been active in international sports and the beach volleyball world for a long time,’ Van den Hoogenband, who won swimming gold medals at the 2000 and 2004 Olympics, told Dutch outlet NOS at the start of the Games.

‘He has played in World Cups, European Championships and World Championships, but then you see that things are different around the Games. That things are exaggerated around the Games.

‘We don’t have blinkers on and we don’t close our eyes. I’m the boss, responsible for the whole team, for the athletes. He’s just a member of the team, he qualified, and that’s why he deserves our support.’
